coredata.dc:title: Surface capacitive charge storage in carbon nanodots-anchored hybrid halide perovskites
coredata.dc:creator: Oloore, Luqman E.
Gondal, Mohammed A.
Popoola, AbdulJelili
Popoola, Idris K.
coredata.dc:description: Surface faradaic process can bring about storage (pseudocapacitive) mechanism that store much more energy than conventional electric double-layer capacitors (ELDCs) quite rapidly. Many transition metal oxides possess pseudocapacitor properties, but only few are capable of providing a high power capability because of their low intrinsic conductivities. Here we demonstrate that lead- and bismuth-based hybrid halide perovskites, anchored with carbon nanodots, can operate at rates exceeding those of ELDCs and provide higher area normalized and volumetric capacitances than standalone halide perovskites and carbon dots. We report carbon nanodots in methyl ammonium bismuth iodide (CNDs/MBI) and methyl ammonium lead iodide (CNDs/MPI) hybrid electrodes, designed to facilitate electron transfer and cation accessibility to faradaic active sites. Devices based on CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI electrodes are able delivered up to 550 Fg−1 and 402 Fg-1, with corresponding energy densities of 76.5 Wh/kg and 55.8 Wh/kg at scan rates of 0.01 V−1. Deconvolution of the cyclic voltanogramm also provide insight to fraction of charge storage due to surface- and diffusion-controlled process.

head.ce:title: Surface capacitive charge storage in carbon nanodots-anchored hybrid halide perovskites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface faradaic process can bring about storage (pseudocapacitive) mechanism that store much more energy than conventional electric double-layer capacitors (ELDCs) quite rapidly. Many transition metal oxides possess pseudocapacitor properties, but only few are capable of providing a high power capability because of their low intrinsic conductivities. Here we demonstrate that lead- and bismuth-based hybrid halide perovskites, anchored with carbon nanodots, can operate at rates exceeding those of ELDCs and provide higher area normalized and volumetric capacitances than standalone halide perovskites and carbon dots. We report carbon nanodots in methyl ammonium bismuth iodide (CNDs/MBI) and methyl ammonium lead iodide (CNDs/MPI) hybrid electrodes, designed to facilitate electron transfer and cation accessibility to faradaic active sites. Devices based on CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI electrodes are able delivered up to 550 Fg−1 and 402 Fg-1, with corresponding energy densities of 76.5 Wh/kg and 55.8 Wh/kg at scan rates of 0.01 V−1. Deconvolution of the cyclic voltanogramm also provide insight to fraction of charge storage due to surface- and diffusion-controlled process.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, organic−inorganic hybrid perovskite materials have emerged as a family of functional energy materials with immense interest in photovoltaic [1–4] and optoelectronics devices [5–7]. This family of materials are also of high interest for technological applications in fields where fast ionic conductors are of particular interest, most notably electrochemical devices such as batteries [8–12], supercapacitors [13–15], fuel cells and electrochromic windows [16–20]. Halide perovskites of the type ABX3, where A is cation such as: Cs+, CH3NH3 +, or HC(NH2)2 +; B = Pb2+, Bi3+or Sn2+; and X = Cl−, Br−, or I−, are known to exhibit prominent electronic conductivity and fast ionic conduction [12,21], and are referred to as mixed electronic – ionic conductors. Owing to the soft character of the compounds, native defects in hybrid halide perovskite materials are known to migrate within the lattice [22]. Hybrid perovskite are also known to behave as materials for lithium ion storage in Li-ion battery electrodes [23] through intercalation/de-intercalation process, thus they have potentials for use in electrochemical devices. Recently, N. Vicente and G. Garcia-Belmonte presented an outstanding Li-ion storage in MAPbBr3 with stable specific capacity of about 200 mA h/g by using it as a battery anode [12]. Similarly, investigation of electrochemical properties of MAPbI3-xBrx mixed halide perovskites thin films as Li-ion batteries electrodes by Ramirez et al. showed that the 3D structure can exhibit up to 160 mAhg−1/500 mAhg−1 charge/discharge capacities [24]. In addition, organometallic halide perovskites MAPbI3 has been employed, with quasi-solid-state electrolytes, as electrodes materials for supercapacitors [25], and presented area normalized capacitances of 21.5 μFcm−2. Furthermore, charge storage mechanism in nanocomposites of MAPbI3 and MA3Bi2I9 with nickel oxide (NiO) has been investigated in a recent work, and the results revealed that; the storage is due to both surface- (psesudocapacitive) and diffusion-controlled processes [13].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are class of carbon materials which have generated tremendous interest in academic and industrial researches, due to their outstanding physicochemical properties such as; good functionality, high aqueous solubility, high stability and excellent electronic conductivity [26–29]. In addition to their applications in sensor and catalysis [30,31], carbon nanodots are found very useful in the area of energy storage, as electrode materials, because of their excellent electronic attributes which enable them to act as both donors and acceptors of electrons [29]. Also, versatility in benefits of the carbon nanodots can be attributed to their cost effectiveness, low dimensionality, small sizes, low toxicity and surface wettability [32,33]. Like porous carbons and activated carbons from biomass [34–40], different forms of CNDs have been employed for energy storage applications such as in batteries and supercapacitors electrodes [41–43]. For example, an investigation by Zhu et al. revealed that RuO2-impregnated carbon quantum dots hybrid electrodes result in an excellent electrochemical performance with high rate capability and cycle life [29]. Another investigation has reported that CNDs/MnO2 nano-flower fabricated by means sonochemical synthesis can give up to 345 Fg-1 specific capacitance [42]. Most of the reports on the application of carbon dots in energy storage have introduced materials with pseudocapacitance properties to improve its capacitance, mainly oxides of transition metals [32,42–44], which are attributed poor electrical conductivities. It would be of great theoretical and practical importance to study whether the individual capacitive properties of CNDs and the perovskite materials will complement each other and produced improved electrochemical properties.

Here we report CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI (where MBI = methyl ammonium bismuth iodide and MPI = methyl ammonium lead iodide) hybrid architectures, designed to facilitate electron transfer and cation accessibility and/or diffusion, towards realization of excellent electrochemical performances with high power density and energy storage in symmetric supercapacitors. Specifically, composites films of MBI and MPI hybrid perovskites, which are composed of cation accessible sites [24], and the CNDs are in situ hierarchically prepared solution processed spin-coating technique. Symmetric supercapacitor devices fabricated from the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI hybrid electrodes and exhibit increased specific capacitances, high rate performance.

Starting with amorphous carbon, carbon dots was prepared by the pulsed laser ablation in liquid (PLAL) technique, as reported in our previous work [45–47]. The source of irradiation for the ablation process was a second harmonic Nd-YAG (Brilliant B) laser, operating at 532 nm wavelength with pulse repetition rate of 10 Hz, a pulse width of 5 ns and a pulse energy of 280 mJ/pulse. Suspension of 0.5 mg activated carbon nanopowder in 15 ml ethanol was ablated by the laser until visible color change, from black to light yellow, was observed. The target material was subjected to continuous stirring during the PLAL process to ensure homogeneous solution and the laser beam was rotated by a prism. The resulting carbon colloidal quantum dots was labelled carbon nanodots (CNDs).

The methyl ammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3) and methyl ammonium bismuth iodide (MA3 Bi2I9) precursors were prepared using one-step solution route. A mixture of methyl ammonium iodide (CH3NH3I) with lead (II) iodide (PbI2) and with bismuth (III) iodide (BiI3) was dissolved in anhydrous N, N- dimethylformamide (DMF) to prepare MAPbI3 and MA3Bi2I9, respectively. Detailed discussion for the precursor preparation is available in our previous works [13,15]. Clear solution of the MAPbI3 and MA3Bi2I9 were labelled MPI and MBI, respectively.

Films of CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI hybrid crystals, on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) were prepared, separately, by spin coating. The glasses cleaned sequentially with soap, deionized water, acetone and ethanol followed by sonication then, heating at 100 °C to remove residual organic solvents. To fabricate CNDs/MBI (MPI) electrodes, 100 μL of the CNDs, followed by 100 μL of MBI (MPI), precursors was spin-coated atop the FTO at 3000 rpm for 30 s, dried for about 5 min and then heated at 75 °C for 15 min, cooled to the room temperature and then removed. As pristine, separate MBI and MPI electrodes were fabricated, using 100 μL of each precursors.

The full cells fabricated by separating two identical electrodes with thin layer of electrolyte. The electrolyte (2 M Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) dissolved in a clear PVA-chlorobenzene solution) was coated on an electrodes, then two identical electrodes were held together by two binder clips. The devices were kept over 48 h before electrochemical measurements are performed, to improve the contact between the electrodes and the electrolytes.

Power X-ray diffraction patterns of the CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI films were collected at room temperature, over the 2θ angular range of 20° – 80°, using a Bruker AXS D-2 PHASER diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.540 Å, 50 kV, and 100 mA) as the X-ray source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα X-rays as the excitation source. The core level spectra were de-convoluted by the avantage software. The morphologies of the films were examined using a Lyra TESCAN field emission scanning electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 20.0 kV.

Electrochemical performances of the devices were measured in a two-electrode configuration. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed in potential windows from 0 to 1.0 V, at various scan rates and applied AC voltage with 10 mV amplitude in frequency range from 105 Hz–10−2 Hz, respectively.

Fig. 1 a depicts the XRD patterns of CNDs/MPI and CNDs/MBI films. The broadening in the XRD patterns centered at about 26° is typical of the CNDs phase, which contains highly disordered carbon with a predominantly amorphous structure [48]. The diffraction patterns of the CNDs/MPI films show characteristic peaks of MAPbI3 orthorhombic lattice (space group I4/m or I4/mcm) [49,50]. The peaks in diffraction patterns of CNDs/MBI are consistent with literature reports [51,52], suggesting the hexagonal lattice system with the P63/mmc space group [52,53]. As shown in Fig. 1b, the use of ABX3 hybrid halide perovskites in energy storage is related to their 3D framework of corner-connected BX6 octahedrons, with A-site cations located between them and multiple channels for cations intake. The morphological investigation of the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI films, shown in Fig. 1 reveals that, addition of carbon dots does not alter the crystalline structures [54] of the MA3Bi2I9 and MAPbI3 phases. As can be seen in Fig. 1d, the CNDs/MPI crystallites grow as narrow micron-sized platelets, typical crystal behaviour of an orthorhombic MAPbI3 lattice phase [15,55]. Similarly, the CNDs/MBI films, as shown in Fig. 1c, form typical micron-sized hexagonal shapes of MA3Bi2I9 crystals.

Fig. 2 a and b shows the survey of X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra, recorded in the binding energy range 0–800 eV, to investigate the chemical states and elemental composition on the surface of CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI films, respectively. The XPS survey spectrum of CNDs/MBI film shows peaks at binding energies of 286.4 eV, 399.3 eV, 533.2 eV for C1s, N1s, O1s, while, for CNDs/MPI film, nitrogen peak is not visible but the peaks of C1s and O1s appears at 284.8 eV and 530.5 eV binding energies respectively. The doublet peaks of I3d, with their associated spin orbit splitting are located at around 620.3 eV and 631.8 eV, respectively, for CNDs/MBI film and at 618.3 eV and 629.8 eV for the CNDs/MPI film. Doublet peaks corresponding to Bi4f are observed around 160.6 eV, 165.9 eV, and those that can be assigned to Pb4f are found at 137.6 eV and 142.6 eV binding energies, along with their spin orbit splitting. C1s binding energy at 284.8 eV and 286.4 eV, for adventitious carbon, was used to calibrate the XPS spectra [56,57]. As shown in Fig. 2c, f, C1s core level spectra was de-convoluted into two peaks at 286.4 eV and 289.9 eV for the CNDs/MBI and, similarly, at 284.8 eV and 286.9 eV for the CNDs/MPI. Peaks at 284.8 and at 286.4 eV belong to common adventitious carbon, and are attributed to C – C [58], and C – O [55], while those at ∼ 289.9 eV belong to carbon bonded to nitrogen (C – N) [59]. Fig. 2g depicts de-convoluted Pb4f core level spectra of the CNDs/MPI films which exhibits symmetrical peaks, corresponding to the spin orbit splitting of the Pb4f7/2 and Pb4f5/2, at 137.6 eV and 142.6 eV respectively, representing the Pb2+ cation in the Methyl ammonium lead iodide structure [60]. An additional peak labelled which appears at around 141.8 eV binding energy is assigned to PbI2 component. Bi4f core level spectra for the CNDs/MBI sample (Fig. 2d) exhibits doublet peaks of Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2 corresponding to its spin orbit splitting at 160.6 eV and 165.9 eV respectively, which can be attributed to the characteristic signals from Bi3+ [54,61]. This indicates that Bi was mainly in the +3 oxidation state and the corresponding satellite peaks appears due to broadening. There are two intense peaks, belonging to I3d5/2 and I3d3/2, exhibited by the XPS for core level I3d for the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI films. These binding components are attributed to triiodide ion I3− [62]. Additional peaks in the CNDs/MPI are assigned to the oxidation of iodine at the surface [63,64].

Supercapacitors were assembled with two identical CNDs/MBI (mass loading of 2.9 mg/cm−2) or CNDs/MPI (mass loading of 4.0 mg/cm−2) films as both anode and cathode, and 2 M Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) dissolved in a clear PVA-chlorobenzene solution as both the electrolyte and the separator, to evaluate practical energy storage performance. The shapes of the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves for CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI electrodes, taken over a potential window of 1.0 V (Fig. 3 a and b) indicate presence of electrochemical energy storage in both anode and cathode of the devices. Anodic redox peaks, which represent the cation intercalation/de-intercalation behavior in the perovskite lattice, occur in CNDs/MPI device at around 0.35 V, for scan rates 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, and 0.20 V/s. The proposed reaction mechanism for this cation insertion process is expressed by the following equation:
(1) ABX3 + γM+ + γe− ↔ABX3Mγ
where M+ is the intercalated cation, proton in this case. However, these redox peaks disappear at higher sweep rates (0.50 and 1.00 V/s), which is believed to be due to kinetic limitation associated with cation diffusion into the lattice at faster sweep rates. The 3D hierarchical and bicontinuous architecture of the composite electrodes, which provides pathways for electron and ion transport, enables maintenance of the shapes of the CV curves at various scan rates from 10 to 1000 mVs−1. Relative to the standalone MBI and MPI electrodes, the CNDs/MBI and CND/MPI hybrid electrodes have remarkably enhanced current density because of distinguish CNDs layer, which, in addition to increased conductivity due to C – C chains for fast, reversible surface redox reactions, facilitate the cation intercalation/deintercalation for more energy storage. The effect of CNDs addition becomes particularly evident when plotting the rate dependence of the charging reaction of the devices as shown in Fig. 3c. For instance, at a sweep time of 50 s, the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI devices have charged up to capacity of 153 and 112 mA h/g while the pristine MBI and MPI are at 28.7 and 34.2 mA h/g, respectively, and are almost fully charged. These results also demonstrate that the CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI exhibit faster charging rates for ion storage than the devices based on standalone MBI and MPI.

Fig. 3d compares the gravimetric (volumetric) capacitances of the CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI devices, calculated from the cyclic voltanogramms, with the values of the capacitances of the MBI and MPI devices at sweep rates 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00 V/s. The specific capacitances were calculated by integrating the CV curves in term of Equation (2):

V , φ, ν, and i represent gravimetric capacitance, volumetric capacitances, potential and sweep rate and current, respectively. V and m are geometric volume and mass of the electrodes. The CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI devices achieve gravimetric (volumetric) capacitances of 550 Fg-1 (610 Fcm−3) and 402 Fg-1 (464 Fcm−3), respectively, at a scan rate of 0.01 V-1. The two devices retain up to capacitances of 187 Fg-1 (207 Fcm−3) and 117 Fg-1 (136 Fcm−3), respectively, at a scan rate of 1.00 V-1. These results are obviously multiple time more than the pristine MBI and MPI electrodes based devices. This outstanding rate performance enlist both CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI electrodes to outperform some supercapacitor of the carbon quantum (nano) dots electrodes or its composite materials, reported in the literature [65–70]. Fig. 1e depicts the practical energy and power densities of devices in a ragone plot. The gravimetric power (PD, in Wkg−1) and energy (ED, in Whkg−1) densities were calculated according to Equations (3) and (4):

The power densities reach about 93.8 kW/kg and 54.7 kW/kg, at scan rate of 1.00 V/s, while the maxima energy densities are about 76.5 Wh/kg and 55.8 Wh/kg, at scan rate 0.01 V/s, for the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI devices respectively. These results are very superior to the values obtained for the standalone devices, as illustrated in the figure, comparable to other carbon-based supercapacitors [65–71] (see Table 1 ). Furthermore, these properties certify the unique capability of carbon nanodots decorated halide perovskites-based electrochemical devices to realize high-density energy storage/delivery at high power rates, which makes them potentially competitive against other electrochemical energy storage devices, such as lead-acid batteries and thin-film lithium batteries, for various high-power applications.

Results of typical electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), for the composite electrodes, are presented in Fig. 3f. The Nyquist plots collected at an applied potential of 10 mV in the frequency range 100 kHz–0.01 Hz are in a good agreement with the CV data. At low frequencies, a low ion diffusion resistance and a sharp, demonstrating the capacitive behaviour of the electrodes, are observed. The diffuse layer resistance region, corresponding to the electric double layer, EDL (or ion diffusion) formation, presents less steep slope for CNDs/MPI than CNDs/MBI devices. This result establish that percentage of capacitive storage in the former may be larger, and that both diffusion-limited and non-diffusion-limited process are present. Also, linear interpolation of low frequency part of the Nyquist plot to where the imaginary impedance axis (Z՛՛) is zero reveals values of equivalent series resistances (ESR) for CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI to be 1.70 Ω and 3.80 Ω, respectively. Using the bulk electrode resistance obtained from the EIS data, the estimated electrical conductivities for the CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI electrodes are

represents the normalized current for an irreversible system as indicated by the cyclic voltammetric response. The b-values can be obtained by linear fitting of the plots of logarithms of the voltammetric current (Log (i)) against the logarithms of the scan rates (Log (v)) at each fixed potential. Representative plots at 0.25 V (anode) & 0.30 V (cathode) for the CNDs/MBI electrodes and 0.25 V (anode) & 0.35 V (cathode) for the CNDs/MPI electrodes are presented in Fig. 4 a. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the b-values range from 0.5 to 1.0, indicating the presence of both the capacitive storage and diffusion-limited processes. The cathodic sweeps present regions where the b-values are ∼1.0, indicating presence of total capacitive current. The b-values variation in the anodic sweep falls in the range 0.6–0.75 for the CNDs/MBI device and mainly between 0.5 and 0.8 for the CNDs/MPI device. We can be infer that mixed diffusion-controlled and capacitive current are present as no clear transition point between the two process is known.

Fig. 4c and d shows linear plots of Equation (8) at various fixed potentials for the CNDs/MBI and the CNDs/MPI devices respectively, which can be fitted to determine the values of κ1 and κ2, to quantify the fractions of current due to each contribution. Though only the curves for the potentials listed on the curves are showing here, the contributions were determined at fixed potentials covering the entire potential window. From this procedure, we are able to distinguish quantitatively between the currents arising from surface (capacitive) process and proton or cation insertion as depicted by the voltage profile in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 – the shaded area represents the capacitive current. As examples, the cyclic voltammograms of the devices based on CNDs/MBI electrodes at sweep rates 0.05, 0.20 and 1.00 V/s are presented in Fig. 5b – c, along with the calculated capacitive contributions to the total current. Similar information, for the CNDs/MPI electrodes-based device at sweep rates 0.20, 0.50 and 1.00 V/s is shown in Fig. 6b – c. All measurements were made in similar way, and quantitative analysis of the current contributions was applied in a specific potential range. Figs. 5a and 6a present percentage contribution to the charge storage by each process. The results indicate that; at high scan rates, the storage is mainly capacitive, while diffusion-limited process dominate at lower scan rates, in both cases. These observations shows that the composite of CNDs and organometallic halide perovskite present higher capacity than individual electrodes without destroying its capacitive property. These results are consistent with those presented earlier, in that the current are due to both the surface and diffusion-controlled intercalation. We calculated the surface area normalized capacitances (CA), in order to determine whether the capacitive storage mechanism arises from double layer or pseudocatacitance effect. The values ranges between 49.8 and 147 mF/cm2 for the CNDs/MBI device and between 43.3 and 149 mF/cm2 for the CNDs/MPI device. These values are several times higher than what one would expect for the double layer capacitance, as compared to the previous findings [25,45,76]. The high values of areal capacitance obtained here suggest that the mechanisms for ion storage at the surface are dominated by pseudocapacitive process.

For practical application, cycling performance of an energy storage device is an important factor; Fig. 7 a shows plot of percentage capacitance retention as a function of the cycle numbers. After 2800 CV cycles, both CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI retain about 99% of the original capacitance. These results show that the two composite electrodes has potentially excellent cycle performance. The rate capability of both CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI are shown in Fig. 7b. Bothe electrodes show capacities of 80 mAhg−1 and 75 mAhg−1 at 1C rate, which drops to 52 mAhg−1 and 32 mAhg−1, respectively, at higher rate of 10C. Apparently, almost all the capacities of CNDs/MBI, at different rates, are higher than those of the CNDs/MPI electrode, demonstrating a better rate capability. For example, although the two composite electrodes retain significant capacities at 10C, the CNDs/MBI electrodes, which retains ∼65% capacity, exhibits a better rate performance than the CNDs/MPI electrodes which only retains ∼43% capacity.

In summary, we have demonstrated how carbon nanodots anchored hybrid halide perovskite, as electrodes for supercapacitor can improve the electrochemical capacitance of standalone hybrid halide perovskites. This has been achieved using CNDs/MBI or CNDs/MPI films as both anode and cathode, and 2 M H3PO4, dissolved in a clear PVA-chlorobenzene solution as both the electrolyte and the separator, with a voltage window of 1.0 V. The electrode designs enabled outstanding capacitance of up to 550 F/g and 402 F/g at 10 mV/s with corresponding energy densities of 76.5 Wh/kg and 55.8 Wh/kg.

We also showed that both surface and diffusion-limited processes are involved in the charge storage mechanism of these electrodes, and distinguished, qualitatively and quantitatively, the fraction of charge stored due to the identified processes. This study revealed that CNDs/MBI and CNDs/MPI can be used as pseudocapacitive materials for energy storage at rates exceeding 1.0 V−1 or higher, opening new exciting opportunities in the fields of electrochemical energy storage.

Luqman E. Oloore: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, preparation. Mohammed A. Gondal: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. AbdulJelili Popoola: Investigation, Visualization. Idris K. Popoola: Investigation, Data curation.
